---
title: "A discussion of Training"
id: whatstraining
author: "Peter Raidl"
date: "2025-04-08"
categories: [blog, science, training, exercise, concept]
image: "modelA.png"
---

```{=html}
<!--# 
* Textbook definition of training
* Comparison to Learning
* Where does our understanding come from -> bio medical literature
* Direct link to stress research
* Older historical ideas about training

What is sports? There are many definitions. In german we use the word Sports for many different movement disciplines not just for clearly structured, performance oriented and competition oriented movement parctices. In every day language we use sports for healthy movement behaviour as well.
I would like to see a little bit more narrow definitions. My definition for sports:
An umbrella term for movement practices that are highly structure and follow certain rules (that are not static) and sports always represents and shapes culure or is politically informed.

Movement in  general ist the spatiotemporal change of physical mass or concepts (like thoughts or ideas). In terms of movement sciences we look at transfer of chemical to kinetic energy within living organisms.
-->
```

Most of my extensive (formal and informal) education falls into the realm of training science. So I want to start right there and give you a introduction to what I mean with *training*.

# What is Training {#sec-training}

When referring to training, I talk about a *process* that is specifically designed to improve, rebuild or stabilize some human **capacity**, **biological function,** or **structure**. Breaking down this working definition, I want to emphasize the term *process*. This means that training can not be defined without inferring to changes over time. Further, a process does involve multiple components of which none is a sufficient substitute for the whole. The components of training might be defined in different ways. I want to create a very simple model (A) and a second model that is a little more sophisticated (B). Keep in mind that the simple model A implicitly comprises many presuppositions. This is still true for model B. Only that model B is more easily revealed as being false.

::: {#fig-TPmodels layout-ncol="2"}
![Model A: Training as a Black Box](modelA.png){#fig-modelA}

![Model B: Training as a function of Demand and Response morated by the capacity](modelB.png){#fig-modelB}

Working Model for Training Processes
:::

Model @fig-modelA shows a classical example of a black box. Training can simply be viewed as an input-output function. The input represents the demand we put on the organism and the output the adaptations that happen. There is some undefined (or undefinable) black "magic" happening in the middle, transforming adaptations from demands. This might sound like a vague model, but it has the advantage of being hard to falsify. Every model of higher granularity also includes more potential breaking points. Model @fig-modelB depicts the different components of the training process. Here, I would generalize them as (1) demand to the organism, (2) capacity of the organism at the given situation, (3) response of the system and (4) mental processing and controlling. These four components stand in a circular relationship with each other.

The mental processing (4) (@fig-modelB) may need further explanation. While most of what we see in biologically happens between nodes (1), (2) and (3), the mental model makes it a training process.

::: callout-note
We call it training if the intention is a systematic link between demand, capacity and adaptive response.

The mental processing is a systemic link, which represents planning, observing and reorganizing the three other nodes.
:::

The training *process* can not be described by just looking at one component. Training is represented as the link function of all those points. So it can not be described as a single event in time, which makes it very hard to measure. For the most part, only the input (the demand) and the output (the adaptations) can be quantified. So there is no real way to measure training itself, only the specific parameters that describe the demand (intensity, volume, frequency, density, intent) and the adaptations we track.

## Three sites of adaptations:

Within the model of training, I would like to list three sites of adaptation: Biological capacities, function, and structures. An example of **capacity** would be the ability to run a marathon or go to the toilet without help. But it could also be the ability to read or write faster or hold one's attention on the breath. With **biological function,** I very broadly refer to the ability to change internal states that can be measured[^1]. A typical example could be the control of heart rate or the blood glucose levels during different demanding situations. Lastly, **biological structure** ended up with a separate category. Here, I refer to preferable changes in tissue size, number of sub-units or architecture like increased muscle volume or number of red blood cells. The interesting thing about how training is typically defined is that it has to be goal-oriented. There is a motivation and a reason to structure one's actions to reach the defined sites of adaptations. Following this textbook-like definition, someone MUST have a conscious, explicit idea of what to work on and how to achieve this! So, there is no such thing as training by chance. Also, adaptions to physical activity that are not led by a systematic approach to reach a goal can not be considered training.

[^1]: This does not mean that we have to measure it during the training process. But it sould be measurable in principle. For example we could measure the transport of oxygen to a target tissue in the lab. But we will not do that for every intervention we plan for every person undergoing the training intervention.

## Training vs other forms of change

We can now compare training to a very similar process. **Learning** can be unconscious. It can happen out of internal or external demands to the system/organism, not just out of long-term planning. Changes occurring by learning should be differentiated from developmental or fatigue-induced adaptations. Obviously, learning can also be pre-contemplated and goal-oriented, just like training. The difference might be that learning involves long-term changes in behaviors or expressing completely new behaviors. Training is sometimes thought of as a process that can only be done after a pattern already emerges, i.e., was already learned. But this is not a strict rule, and maybe we could define training as a subcategory of learning.

The funny thing about the difference between training and learning is that the predetermined goal that is inherent in training does not have to be conscious to the organism performing the training. A rat or a pigeon could also be trained without it understanding the training process. Actually, before the focus on physiological adaptation was popular, training principles were also heavily influenced by psychology. This was during a time when behaviorism was the proper way to frame human learning[^2] [@krugerTrainingTheoryWhy2006]. Training principles for a human (or a rat, as the most influential scientists of that time didn't see much of a difference) were radically different from today's viewpoint. A person could be trained by different forms of conditioning without the person being aware of it. The scientist or coach could play god in this scenario and transform the person into "whatever they like"[^3]. Still, somebody has to be aware of the training process. A religious person believing in a *greater plan* that unfolds might call any learning process also training. But for the rest of us, there is a difference.

[^2]: Especially in the USA. In Europe *Gestalt Psychology* and other phenomenological approaches were more prominent, as far as I understand.

[^3]: Watson famously claimed to be able to make anything out of a healthy infant

## Measure and manage

This all leads us back to the problem of tracking expected and unexpected changes in capacity, function, and structure. Typically, we only measure the thing we previously specified as important. Surely, there are more general ways to measure ANY changes in the categories previously defined. But this data-mining approach to tracking, which often comes up naturally in practitioners, can actually not fall under the umbrella of training-induced changes. One could even argue that cross-training effects are a complete misnomer. If you train to get stronger in lifting weights and after a couple of weeks you realize you have become a better runner, this can not be a training effect by most textbooks. The definition of training seems to be very much in line with a deductive, hypothesis-driven way of doing science. Clearly, we do not handle it like that and mix it up with inductive methods of observing post-hoc what happened, to reason the effects of the training process. I think this is actually a good thing. Certainly, historically, we mostly understood training principles from an inductive perspective. We built our ideas on how to train by observing changes time and time again.
